coredata.dc:title: Time-dependent characterization of graphene quantum dots and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal methods
coredata.dc:creator: Nxele, Siphesihle Robin
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: We report on the facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs) by the bottom-up hydrothermal synthetic process. The time is varied to study its effects on the structural, hydrodynamic and optical properties of these nanostructures. The techniques used to characterize these nanostructures were transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometry, dynamic light scattering, and zetametry (zeta potential), and by energy dispersive X-ray, UV/vis, fluorescence, Fourier transform infrared, Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies. For gCNQDs and NGQDs, Raman spectroscopy showed an increase in disorder with synthesis time, indicating introduction of more triazine groups for the former and increase in doping with the N atoms for the latter, hence higher temperatures are recommended. For GQDs, Raman spectra showed an increase in the spatial order of the π-conjugated structure with synthesis time. Considering all the techniques employed in this work, the synthesis times of 6h and 8 h are recommended for GQDs and NGQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
Optimisation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta (ζ) potential

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ) as well as Rhodes University /DST Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (200 nm scale) (A) with distribution curves (B) and EDX spectra (C) of GQDs at 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h synthesis.
Summary of TEM-determined size evolution with time of each quantum dots sample.
DLS plots of (a) GQDs, (b) NGQDs and (c) gCNQDs in water. (d) A plot of hydrodynamic size vs synthesis time is also shown.

A selection of ground state electronic absorption and emission spectra of GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs in water. Excitation wavelength = 350 nm; conc. = 10 mg/mL (gCNQDs) and 5 mg/ mL (GQDs and NGQDs). Fl = Fluorescence, Abs = absorbance.
XRD patterns of GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs. The NGQDs 8 h plot is shown as an insert as it overlaps with the 2 h plot.
FTIR spectrum of GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs.
Raman spectra of synthesized GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs.
AFM topographic profiles for gCNQDs. Inserts: Height profiles of each QDs samples.
XPS wide scans of GQDs and high resolution deconvoluted spectra of GQDs (C1s).
Synthetic route of (A) gCNQDs, (B) GQDs and (C) NGQDs.
Summary of DLS sizes and polydispersity indices (PDI) of synthesized quantum dots in water. Values in brackets are sizes from TEM.
Summary of Zeta (ζ) potential data, mobility and conductivity of synthesized quantum dots in water.
Summary of absorption and emission spectral data values of synthesized quantum dots in water with peak assignments.
XPS C1s, deconvoluted peak assignments (binding energies in eV) of GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs.
XPS N1s deconvoluted peak assignments (binding energies in eV) of NGQDs and gCNQDs.
Summary of % Oxygen and Carbon present in the GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs synthesized at 2, 4, 6 and 8 h.

head.ce:title: Time-dependent characterization of graphene quantum dots and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal methods

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NGQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs) by the bottom-up hydrothermal synthetic process. The time is varied to study its effects on the structural, hydrodynamic and optical properties of these nanostructures. The techniques used to characterize these nanostructures were transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometry, dynamic light scattering, and zetametry (zeta potential), and by energy dispersive X-ray, UV/vis, fluorescence, Fourier transform infrared, Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies. For gCNQDs and NGQDs, Raman spectroscopy showed an increase in disorder with synthesis time, indicating introduction of more triazine groups for the former and increase in doping with the N atoms for the latter, hence higher temperatures are recommended. For GQDs, Raman spectra showed an increase in the spatial order of the π-conjugated structure with synthesis time. Considering all the techniques employed in this work, the synthesis times of 6h and 8 h are recommended for GQDs and NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based quantum dots have gained attention in medical research due to their biologically-suitable characteristics such as low toxicity [1], biocompatibility [2], chemical and photo-stability [3,4], good solubility in water [5] and their ability to be used as multifunctional nanoplatforms. In addition, their optical, electrical, chemical and structural properties can be tuned to suit specific functions [6]. With the improvements and developments in biomedicine and the need for therapeutic drugs that are not harmful to the biological systems, there is a great need to have a holistic understanding of these nanostructures in terms of their functionality and behaviour in different environments. Although research has been and is still going on aimed at understanding these nanostructures and their functionality, most findings have been speculative and theoretical [7–10]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been synthesized using various methods in an attempt to produce batches that are as close to being monodispersed as possible with little to no aggregation. This is to ensure that each quantum dot is exposed for conjugation with other materials. It has been shown that varying parameters such as temperature and time have an effect on characteristics such as size, number of QDs layers, and extent of functionalization which in turn affects optical properties which are crucial for biological studies. The work reported herein looks at three different types of quantum dots, namely, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (gCNQDs), and the effects of synthesis time on their characteristics, more specifically their physical and optical properties. It is also necessary to have these nanostructures water soluble so as to apply them in biological studies and the functional groups will assist in this. Barely any work has gone into the optimization of graphene-based quantum dots. Some recent reports have shown optimization of quantum dots but only focusing on their fluorescent properties [11,12]. Another report looked at the effect of synthesis time on carbon dots synthesized from glucose using a wide time range which yielded useful results [13], however herein the use of different precursors is investigated and NGQDs and gCNQDs are included in the study. Moreover, a wider range of analytical techniques are used and compared in this work to obtain an in-depth view of how the changing synthesis time affects the structural formation of GQDs and their derivatives. Even though the effect of synthesis time on the nature of the carbon dots has been reported [13], the current work presents an in-depth characterization of the QDs at different synthesis times using a series of techniques. For the first time we explore not just the effect of synthesis time on the size of the QDs, but also the effect on the charge, spatial order, and nature of the functional groups. This work presents concrete recommendations on the best synthesis time for the synthesis of QDs with desired properties.

With that said, we report for the first time, on the optimization of the synthetic routes for GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs, focusing specifically on the reaction time, by the combination of complementary characterization methods. The synthetic pathways of these three graphene QDs were optimized to reduce the polydispersity and control the particle sizes; and their physico-chemical characterization allowed a deeper understanding of their structure and behaviour as potential nanoplatforms. Hydrothermal synthesis was employed in this work which is a more controlled method compared to pyrolysis for example, where various solvents may be used, over a range of temperatures and reaction times [14].
The equipment items employed in this work may be found in the Supporting Information.

Diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN), ethylenediaminotetraacetic acid (EDTA), sodium hydroxide pellets, citric acid and urea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultra-pure water was obtained from a Milli-Q Water system from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, Flex system (Marlow, UK). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from commercial suppliers.

The synthetic route to obtain the gCNQDs (Scheme 1A) has been reported [15]. Briefly: diaminomaleonitrile (1 g, 9.25 mmol), EDTA (0.5 g, 1.34 mmol), and NaOH (5 mL of 1 M) were added to 100 mL of Millipore water and the mixture stirred for 1 h to ensure complete dispersion of the compounds. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 400 mL capacity Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and was heated at 200 °C for 2, 4, 6 and 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown-green product was filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose membrane, followed by the addition of ethanol. The product was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 min. The collected solid was re-dispersed in Millipore water and further dialyzed against ultrapure Millipore water for 24 h to get rid of excess starting materials and by-products. The synthetic routes used to obtain GQDs (Scheme 1B) and NGQDs (Scheme 1C) have also been reported in literature [16]. Briefly for GQDs, citric acid (4.2 g, 1 mmol) and NaOH (2.4 g, 3 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 400 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160 °C for 2, 4, 6 and 8 h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The solid was re-dispersed in water and was dialyzed for two days using a dialysis membrane (MW 1.5 kDa) to remove excess salts. The NGQDs were prepared as described above for pristine GQDs, using urea (3.6 g, 3 mmol) in place of NaOH.

The various carbon QDs were synthesized as reported in literature [15,16] and characterized using a number of techniques. Note that the synthesis times were varied at 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h for optimization in an attempt to obtain the best QDs samples with good dispersity for further applications in biological studies. All other parameters were kept the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to obtain a visual representation of the dispersity of the quantum dots at 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h synthesis times for each type of QDs. Note that the dispersity observed here is of the particles in their solid form after drying on the copper grid. Fig. 1A shows the TEM images of the GQDs with the corresponding distribution curves. For 2 and 4 h, aggregation is observed making it difficult to estimate particle size but there were non-aggregated particles that could be measured. After synthesis for 6 h, better dispersity of particles and less aggregation is observed. After 8 h of synthesis, there is clear spatial distribution of the particles and good dispersity. The particle sizes also seem to not vary significantly for 2 h, 4 h, 6 h synthesis, which is desirable, Table 1 . The average size for 8 h synthesis GQDs is about 4.9 nm with a few larger particles (which could also be aggregates). EDX on the other hand is a qualitative elemental analysis technique, Fig. 1B. The expected elements for GQDs are observed at all reaction times in the EDX images.

The TEM images (Fig. S1A, Supporting Information, SI) for NGQDs obtained at 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h reaction times look similar with good particle dispersity and minimal aggregation. This also implies that even running the reaction up to 8 h does not result in major differences with regards to the particle sizes obtained and the particle dispersity. The EDX for the NGQDs (Fig. S1B, SI) also shows the presence of nitrogen which is the first indication that the GQDs were doped successfully with this element, since nitrogen was absent in the EDX of GQDs. The TEM images for gCNQDs showed aggregation of the samples at 2 h, 4 h and 6 h reaction times, Fig. S2A, SI. After 8 h synthesis, there is improved dispersity of the particles. The EDX of the gCNQDs showed the expected elemental peaks for carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, Fig. S2B, SI. Fig. 2 then illustrates pictorially how the size evolves as synthesis time is varied. For the GQDs the size is consistent at 2, 4 and 6 h then the measured size drops at 8 h synthesis. The trend followed by the NGQDs is an increase in size from 2 h to 4 h then the measured size decreases at 6 h synthesis then a slight increase in measured size at 8 h synthesis. The trend followed by the gCNQDs is a slight increase from 2 h to 6 h then decrease again at 8 h slightly as well. This indicates the minor differences between the measurements of obtained for the gCNQDs at 4 h, 6 h and 8 h. These results clearly show that changing synthesis time results in a change in the size of the nanostructures. With the GQDs, increasing synthesis time within the range studied, results in a decrease in size which may indicate better QDs formation and an increase in sp2 carbon. With the NGQDs and gCNQDs, there is no straightforward trend, but the smaller size measured was between 6 and 8 h synthesis time. This may indicate as well that there is better formation of the QDs structure at longer synthesis times, and possible less aggregation. Therefore, from the results from TEM the synthesis time of 8 h is recommended for the synthesis of GQDs, NGQDs, and gCNQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta (ζ) potential
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the hydrodynamic size of the QDs synthesized, DLS was used. Each QDs sample was dissolved in water and sonicated to ensure the solutions are as homogenous as possible. Fig. 3 shows the DLS plots of each type of QDs samples after 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h synthesis with the particle sizes as well as the polydispersity index (PDI) values summarized in Table 1.

For the GQDs (Fig. 3a, Table 1), a decrease in particle size is observed with longer reaction times. However, the polydispersity indices seem to be very high (higher than 0.5) except for the GQDs synthesized for 8 h. Again 8 h synthesis time is recommended. These findings corroborate what was observed in TEM where the less polydispersed samples proved to be the 8 h sample. Moreover, the particle sizes determined via DLS were different from TEM analysis at 2 h, 4 h and 6 h synthesis times, Table 1. At 8 h synthesis time, the sizes obtained in DLS and TEM were similar. The diameters recorded in DLS are expected to be higher than those measured in TEM, due to hydrodynamic contributions. The TEM size obtained at 2 h synthesis was smaller than that obtained in DLS, which may be a result of the addition of the hydrodynamic radius. At 4 and 6 h synthesis times the diameters measured in TEM were larger than those determined from DLS. The TEM images at 4 and 6 h synthesis show aggregated QDs, which may explain why the measured diameters would be larger than those in DLS.

Looking at the NGQDs results (Fig. 3b), there is no trend observed when relating the reaction times with the particle sizes, Table 1. The sizes do not vary too much from each other (except for 4 h), however, the lowest PDI is found to be 0.51 for the 6 h reaction time. The minor differences between particle sizes for this sample correlates with the information obtained in TEM where there was little deviation in particle size, except for 4 h. Comparing the particle size obtained in DLS to that obtained in TEM analysis, the size differences vary with no observable trend between the synthesis times.

For the DLS results for gCNQDs (Fig. 3c), the lowest size and PDI observed in DLS was after 2 h reaction time. However, the size obtained from TEM for 2 h is larger than that obtained by DLS, which may be due to aggregated samples. Thereafter the sizes seem to increase as well as the PDI values. At 4 h, 6 h and 8 h reaction times this may be explained by the hydrodynamic radius due to the QDs being in water. The sizes obtained for these times in TEM are lower than that obtained in DLS which may be due to the fact that the QDs are measured in solution in DLS but dry and solid in TEM. The relationship between the synthesis time and hydrodynamic size is also shown to better illustrate the trends observed, Fig. 3d.

Table 2 shows zeta potential, conductivity and mobility data of the as-synthesized quantum dots recorded in-solution using the zetasizer. The zeta potential values for all the quantum dots were found to be negative implying negatively charged particles in solution. This is also an indication of the presence of deprotonated carboxylic acid groups at the surface of these nanostructures. Moreover, particles are said to be stable when they have a high zeta potential value (negative or positive). With this information in mind, the GQDs synthesized for 8 h have the highest stability compared to those GQDs synthesized for less hours which are in agreement with previous findings indicating that the GQDs at synthesis time of 8 h is the best batch of QDs.

For the NGQDs also, the sample at 8 h synthesis happens to have the highest zeta potential indication better hydrodynamic stability and the gCNQDs at 6 h synthesis shows the higher value implying better hydrodynamic stability compared to the other synthesized gCNQDs although they also have high zeta potential values. Thus our studies, higher reaction times (6 h or 8 h) are recommended.

The conductivity was also recorded from the zetasizer. In the GQDs and NGQDs batches, the samples synthesized at 8 h synthesis time had the highest conductivity. For the gCNQDs samples, the batches at 8 h synthesis times showed the worst conductivity.

For all QDs, the mobility values obtained were negative which correspond to the zeta potential data which indicate the presence of negatively charged particles. The lower values imply smaller sizes of particles reaching the detector at a faster rate.

Fig. 4 shows the absorption and emission spectra of the GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs in water after synthesis for 2, 4, and 6 h (for GQDs and gCNQDs), 2 and 4 h for NGQDs. The resulting absorption and emission values are summarized in Table 3 . Graphene or graphitic quantum dots are known to possess fluorescent properties which are exploited for various applications [17–21]. The first spectra shown are those attributed to GQDs synthesized at 2, 4, and 6 h. The absorption peak maxima is observed at 337 nm for the 2, 6 and 8 h synthesis. A slight but insignificant shift to lower wavelengths is observed for the GQDs synthesized for 4 h with the peak maxima at 333 nm. These peaks are characteristic of the π-π* transition of the sp2 domains [22]. The emission peak for all GQDs samples was observed at 441 nm with no peak shifts observed.

For the NGQDs, no shifts or intensity changes were observed for both the absorbance and emission spectra for all synthesis times. The absorption peak maxima were observed at 339 nm which is in the same region of those reported in literature [23,24]. Another broad, less intense absorption peak is observed at 595 nm and requires further in-depth studies to determine which transitions it may be attributed to. Usually peaks at this wavelength are observed for SN doped GQDs. The emission spectra for the NGQDs showed peak maxima of 446 nm upon excitation at 350 nm.

For the gCNQDs, characteristic peak around 260 nm is observed which has been attributed to the electronic transition (π-π*) of the s-triazine units of the carbon nitride group [25–28]. No wavelength shifts were observed between synthesis times implying that the optical properties were barely affected in this synthesis time range. The emission spectra for the gCNQDs in water show peak maxima around 450 nm for all samples after excitation at 350 nm.

The structural characteristics of the synthesized quantum dots were also analyzed using XRD as shown in Fig. 5 . All diffractograms appeared broad with a double peak between 2θ = 30 and 40°which is an indication of the presence of graphitic structures [29,30]. Two diffraction peaks corresponding to the {002} (lower 2θ value) and {100} (higher 2θ value) planes of graphitic framework were observed [31]. The peak below 2θ = 20° for NGQDs and gCNQDs is typical of {001} plane of graphite oxide (GO), which is attributed to an increase in sp3 layers spacing and is usually observed for nitrogen doped graphitic material [31]. The broadness of the XRD pattern of GQDs contrasts the sharp peak observed for other carbon materials such as graphite, which shows a sharp (002) peak [30]. The broadness of the peaks on the other hand is characteristic of the small sizes of the QDs [32] which were also observed in the TEM images and results obtained from the DLS data. Their amorphous nature is also another factor that affects the broadness of the peaks.

Fig. 6 illustrates the FTIR spectra of the gCNQDs, GQDs and NGQDs. The spectra of the GQDs and NGQDs are characterized by the vibrations observed between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 typical of the O-H/N-H vibration, 1262 cm−1 which is attributed to the CC bond and lastly 1430 cm−1 which is characteristic of the CO bond. These findings also correspond to those documented in literature [16].

For the gCNQDs, vibrations observed at around 774 cm−1 are characteristic of the heptazine units of the gCNQDs, 1415 cm−1 and 1315 cm−1 peaks are characteristic of the C=N/C-N bonds, 1627 cm−1 peak is characteristic of the CO of the carboxylic acid groups and finally the broad peak between 3715 and 2985 cm−1 characteristic of the N-H/O-H groups. Literature reports also show similar findings and therefore in agreement with the peak assignments made in this work [32–34].

Laser Raman spectral technique was used to determine the quality of as-synthesized GQDs, NGQDs and the gCNQDs as shown in Fig. 7 . A typical Raman spectrum for GQDs show two prominent peaks denoted the G (for sp2 hybridized groups) and D bands (sp3 hybridized groups). There was no clear trend when it came to the Raman shifts of the peaks. An increase in the D band intensities relative to the G band suggests introduction of surface defects, and the degree of these defects is calculated by the ratio of the D and G- bands intensities (ID/IG). Noticeable changes in the ID: IG (sp
3
: sp
2 ) ratio with change in synthesis time were observed for each type of quantum dots sample analyzed, Table 4 . The ID:IG (sp
3
: sp
2 ) ratio is generally considered as a quality parameter to determine the extent of functionalization of the nanomaterials. This is because the G-band is not affected by defects, whereas the D-band is enhanced by the presence of sp3 defects in the sp2 lattice.

For the gCNQDs, the ratios increase with increasing synthesis time, except for 4 h and 6 h, which are the same. This may indicate the presence of more triazine groups forming as the synthesis progresses. For the GQDs on the other hand, the ratios decrease with increasing synthesis time. This is expected as it implies that with time, there is an increase in the spatial order of the π-conjugated structure. Hence higher reaction times are recommended for GQDs.

The ratios obtained for NGQDs also increase with increasing synthesis time indicating an increase in the extent of doping with the nitrogen atoms. The longer the synthesis takes place, the greater the extent of doping of the graphene structure, resulting in more disorder spatially. These results may also indicate the formation of more sheets as quantum dots form layered structures when synthesized.

Atomic force microscopy is another surface analysis technique that is used to determine the structural characteristics of molecules, Fig. 8 , S3 (in SI). In this work, it was used to determine the differences between QDs samples with respect to number of layers as well as thickness as single-layered graphene oxide sheets have the thickness of 0.34 nm by AFM measurements [35]. The size ranged from 0.05 to 0.2 nm for all QDs. These values are lower than a single layer of graphene. This is not a common occurrence but is possible as reported in literature [36]. It is explained in terms of the relationship between the AFM tip and the sample whereby the AFM cannot decipher the difference in force resulting from the chemical nature and/or mechanical nature of the surface or a change in gradient of the topography due to the size of the sample. This may result in compromising the size measured. Once the size of a feature becomes smaller than the effective area of interaction between the AFM tip and sample, the measured height is compromised. As a result, a loss in measured height can amount to up to ∼90% for nanostructures such as QDs. It is important to note, however, that this loss in measured height does not imply that the sample has been damaged or compromised in any way.

For the NGQDs (Fig. S4A, SI), the wide scans only showed peaks corresponding to the nitrogen (N1s), carbon (C1s) and oxygen (O1s) with no peaks due to contaminants present. The C1s (Table 5A) and N1s (Table 5B ) peaks were further analyzed by plotting and deconvoluting the high resolution scans (Fig. S4A, SI). Under the C1s scan, three prominent peaks were observed at binding energies of 286 eV, 284 eV and 282 eV corresponding to O-C=O, C-N/C=N and the sp2 hybridized C-C/C=C, respectively. For the high resolution N1s scan, four peaks were observed at binding energies of 401 eV, 400 eV, 399 eV and 398 eV corresponding to N-C=O, NH, CN and C-N-C bonds, respectively, showing the presence of these bonds in NGQDs.

Fig. S4B, (SI) shows the wide scans as well as the high resolutions scans obtained after analysing the gCNQDs samples. The wide scans show the expected O1s, N1s and C1s peaks. The deconvoluted scans are shown by Fig. S4B. The deconvoluted C1s scans of the gCNQDs samples synthesized at 2 h, 6 h and 8 h revealed three peaks at binding energies of 294 eV, 293 eV and 291 eV which correspond to CO, CN and C-C/C-N, respectively. The binding energies of the peaks observed for the sample at 4 h synthesis time were observed at 289 eV, 287 eV and 285 eV. The same trend is observed for the N1s deconvoluted scans where for the samples obtained at 2 h, 6 h and 8 h synthesis time peaks were observed at binding energies of 399 eV, 397 eV and 396 eV corresponding to C-N-H, N(C)3 and C-N=C, respectively. For the sample reacted for 4 h, the peak observed at 404 eV corresponds to N-oxide, the charging effects in the cyano group and heterocycles or π-excitations [37]. The peak at 401 eV corresponds to the quaternary nitrogen bonded terminal amino group (C–H–N) and 399 eV corresponds to the tertiary nitrogen bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N(C)3 bonds.

Table 6 shows the % oxygen and carbon present in the GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs synthesized at 2, 4, 6 and 8 h. For the GQDs and NGQDs samples, there was no observable trend between the values obtained which may indicate very little difference between samples synthesized at all the reaction times. However, the gCNQDs showed an increase in % oxygen as synthesis time increased and a decrease in % carbon. This may imply an introduction of more triazine groups within the gCNQDs structure as well as more -COOH groups in the periphery.

GQDs, NGQDs and gCNQDs were synthesized successfully at different synthesis times. We show that changing the synthesis time has an effect on the structural makeup and in-solution behaviour of these nanostructures. For GQDs the lowest measured size was observed at 8 h synthesis with the lowest polydispersity index (PDI) value indicating good dispersity. For NGQDs on the other hand, the particles were less polydispered at 6 h synthesis which makes this batch ideal for in-solution applications. This time is also recommended for gCNQDs. Raman spectra verified the presence of defects in the NGQDs structure after doping GQDs as well as the defective nature of the internal structure of the gCNQDs. Zetametry, FTIR and XPS measurements justified the theoretical structures where the dominant function is the COOH group, which would result in deprotonation and overall negative charge in the appropriate solution.
Siphesihle Robin Nxele: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing of original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, reviewing, editing, funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108751.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 1. J. Shen, Y. Zhu, X. Yang and C. Li, Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices, Chem. Commun. (2012) 48, 3686-3699.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of a photo-degradation- resistant quantum dot–polymer composite plate for use in the fabrication of a high-stability white-light-emitting diode
Nanotechnology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 2. E-P. Jang, W-S. Song, K-H. Lee, H. Yang, Preparation of a photo-degradation- resistant quantum dot–polymer composite plate for use in the fabrication of a high-stability white-light-emitting diode, Nanotech. 4 (2013) 957-4484.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 3. D. Qu, M. Zheng, P. Du, Y. Zhou, L. Zhang, D. Li, H. Tan, Z. Zhao, Z.Xie, Z. Sun, Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts, Nanoscale, 5 (2013) 12272-12277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vivo imaging of quantum dots encapsulated in phospholipid micelles
Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: 4. B. Dubertret, P. Skourides, D.J. Norris, V. Noireaux, A.H. Brivanlou, A. Libchaber, In Vivo Imaging of Quantum Dots Encapsulated in Phospholipid Micelles, Science, 298 (2002) 1759-1762.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
J. Mater. Chem.
